What gets me is not his audacity.  What gets me is that gaming media and fellow industry "pros" all parrot this same arrogant hypocrisy. After crowing how great their products are and they dont need "the chuds" as customers... moment those modern audiences dont materialize, they scream at "the chuds" to save their jobs.  

They dont learn and dont care to learn yet continue to dodge all responsibility for the cycle they're in.  Not one of the major media are calling this bad behavior out, not one gaming pro will admit they were wrong.  How do they expect business to improve if they refuse to listen?

author: @zander3100
text:
This has happened TWICE in less than a year. 
And they still won't learn. Marathon is the next talking point and recently a Blizzard developer (you know, that company full of raepers) dunked on someone for **gasp** posting steamcharts data??

It's almost like the industry *needs* marathon to become a hit, and everyone in the medium made it a mission to shield it from any criticism and discourse, potential customers be damned. Little do they know, they could use that time online to improve the game they currently work at. Productivity: crazy concept, I know

So yeah, expect this same narrative to keep going with both marathon, and 2XKO (another GaaS from Tencent) to a lesser degree. Customers do not want bad live service games and the industry isn't having it. So less so that they started to lash out and antagonize the ones that could end up buying their products if they were actually good.

"I restored my account before the one month no-recover policy..."
Because you're addicted, have main character syndrome and think you're more important than you are. 

Does anyone else miss the time when we didn't know these people outside of the odd interview in a magazine? Instead they're terminally online thinking they're some deities. 

I can imagine him sweating while the month counted down, knowing full well he's going to reactivate his account because he's just that important.

author: @elcactusdelamuerte506
text:
F this dude, but at the same time I saw people sending him death threats (both at him and his family) and using his autism as a weapon. I'm assuming by dark corners he's pointing to that kind of thing being directed at him, other staff and the studio the second the game was revealed as the dark corner of the internet rather than people making memes and saying it's a bad game.

Again not justifying his behaviour, but he has a point about how some people behave.

replies.text: are the death threats in the room with us right now?

replies.author: @elcactusdelamuerte506
replies.text:
​@Spectrum0122It did? I saw multiple posts, comments e.t.c. And there was likely much much more, I never really looked much into the story I watched Kiras vids and occasionally had it pop up on feeds across multiple sites. 

Unfortunately we live in a world where people on YouTube with 100s of views end up getting multiple deaththreats because they did a toy ranking and put toy x above toy y. Nvm something that had a larger audience and much more dislike/disinterest.

My confusion is how you can see this man acting in this delusion over the top manner and likely watched the many many videos on this channel of people equally if not more unhinged and how randos online have reacted to them. And yet somehow my claim which is provable by spending a minute on Google, reddit or twitter looking for it is somehow this impossible thing that totally never happened. So either A you have too much trust and hope in the Internet and humanity in general or B you have a blinding hatred of this guy you can't have anyone say anything that might make him seem slightly less of an ass. Which again my stance is this guys an ass and should get off the Internet. And really who cares enough about some nobody game Dev to get that butthurt to hate him to that extreme... Well apart from those mentally ill people sending death threats.

author: @venn2001ad
text: These devs made a mediocre game that was undeservingly hyped up during the award show, only to get appropriately unwelcome reception at its release. And yet, unfortunately, they STILL don't seem to  understand that they were the ones who made the game that way and ultimately set it up for its failure, not the gamers. It doesn't take a genius to realize that all they had to do was to make a good game that the customer base would have wanted... but I guess these devs were no geniuses.

Also LOL at that comment about how supposedly all game discourse is "dead game"/"utter trash", regardless of how good a game is. I don't see that kind of discourse around, say, Slay the Spire 2 or Resident Evil Requiem (other than perhaps some stray comments).

author: @GambitsEnd
text: There was some potential in Highguard. Unfortunately, they kept the game completely secret while not having any testing or public showcase so they could gather feedback and iterate at an earlier stage. Result being the game came out half-baked with a confusing mess of conflicting game design choices. Issues which would have been immediately identified with the possibility of resolution BEFORE release if they had any sort of early feedback. In other words, Highguard was a very rookie game project and suffered from it.

author: @tvrkm6897
text: Things accelerated Highguard's failure? If anything I'd think the Gaming Awards spotlight and controversy is why its failure was slowed down to outlive Concord. Nothing went well for this game, but at least its name got out through it. It could have released to a quiet, quick death, or it could have and did choose the very public but moderately slower death of truly being Concord 2. The only way to make a new option would have been to actually acknowledge there could be problems instead of doing the whole echo chamber of toxic positivity thing. They thought they were good enough to buy a Gaming Awards spotlight they didn't deserve, and now have a spotlight with the Darwin Awards. How many companies are going to refuse to learn from others' mistakes only to refuse to learn from their own?

author: @RaxusXeronos
text:
"The internal pre-reveal feedback" THIS is the problem. it was ALL internal before the public reveal. This game needed to have public playtests before release. These praises did not sound like unbiased sources. They all even admitted that they were going off their pedagree of being Apex and Titanfall devs and that they thought they could just shadow drop it before the whole games awards fiasco happened and they went for the end of show slot.

This game would have flopped HARDER if it wasn't for that trailer showing people it even existed lol. And when many people actually played it, they found out what public playtests would have shown months before. That their game was mid and not worth dropping existing games of a similar type.

author: @chapelknight951
text: It wasn't "discourse from dark corners." It was simply that it didn't look that interesting from the trailer and the actual game was not exciting enough. There were many people who just wanted to hate on the game, too. But, the game itself couldn't stand on it's own and the studio and it's owners abandoned the game in a couple of weeks instead of improving the game. Going nose-to-the-grindstone and making it a focused and fun game would've been far, FAR better.

author: @seankeimakiuchi8055
text:
Joshiepoo deserves to be in the same frame with Concord, Marathon and Highguard on "Trilogia Concord" meme. I nominate him riding that weird animal thing under the three games on that same meme. Fitting though, don't y'all think? He gets to be the one riding an animal as a part of the "Trilogia Concord" meme, immortalizing his presence inside of THE legendary 3 generational failures of game industry. Despite after everything that happened around Highguard and Highguard's "dark corners" like Wildlight devs' whole failed attempt of disguising as indie devs with all the false narrative ("fully self-funded" bullshit) and in reality they were FULLY Tencent-funded, and even some time has passed since the whole red-handed devs being exposed scandal, creating the absolute "zen" moment for all the chuds feeling vindicated because of the REAL "dark corners" were exposed and all the chuds called them out. 

Joshiepoo, your return on Twitter and your very post actually created a beautiful godly vision of "irony x karma" crossover. MAN, I'd feel so genuinely royally embarrassed that if I were you, I might've just dropped everything and go sign up to become a buddhist monk, fully committing the rest of my life into the discipline and teachings of buddhist monk. Maybe you could earn back our respect and admiration if you actually did that and streamlined your buddhist journey as a monk on your Twitter, instead of doing whatever this was. Thought your totally unexpected "return on Twitter" out of nowhere bombshell post would make everybody aware of your presence? AGAIN, bro....again..........literally, who asked?

Literally, bro............just go migrate over to bluesky, I genuinely think you'd fit in that space more comfortably than Twitter.

author: @Yoxiv
text:
"Mental health"
My brother in Christ, you MADE A VIDEO GAME, not go to war. Sure, there have been cases of game developments that have been very intense, but I seriously doubt Highguard's development was remotely taxing. 
Does it suck when you make something and it gets negative feedback? Sure? But saying it's affecting your mental health sounds like a massive exaggeration. Like something a moody teenager would say because of their weekend homework cutting into their free time because of poor time management. These people must have never had a hard day in their lives, so they act like a product they made failing is the end of their very existence.
